did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, and ResNet-18) combined with image processing techniques (median filter and Canny edge detection) to screen for malignant melanoma in digital images. The ResNet-18 model achieved 86.11% accuracy in classifying melanoma vs. normal skin using the MED-NODE dermatology dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a web application for screening malignant melanoma using deep learning models (AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, and ResNet-18) and image processing techniques. It reports new results, including model performance metrics (e.g., 86.11% accuracy for ResNet-18) and user evaluation results from a study with 30 participants, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Web Application for Screening Malignant Melanoma in Digital Images Using Deep Learning
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Big Data and Smart Computing (BigComp)
brief_summary: This study developed a web application for self-screening malignant melanoma using deep learning models and image processing techniques. The researchers evaluated AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, and ResNet-18 on a dataset of 358 images from the MED-NODE dermatology database, combining them with median filters and Canny edge detection. The ResNet-18 model paired with a median filter achieved the highest performance, yielding 86.11% accuracy, 88.10% precision, 88.10% sensitivity, and 83.33% specificity. The web application was subsequently evaluated by 30 participants, including general practitioners, who reported high satisfaction with an overall rating of 4.48 out of 5. The results demonstrate the potential of deep learning and median filters to facilitate effective early detection of melanoma risk.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the screening web application. The study specifically excluded amelanotic malignant melanoma from the dataset due to lack of pigmentation. The dataset contained 216 images of melanoma skin cancer (146 from MED-NODE and additional images collected).
The negative class used for binary classification. The dataset contained 142 images of normal skin (100 from MED-NODE and additional images collected).

Mentioned in the context of image collection criteria and clinical features to distinguish from melanoma. The paper notes that images of other skin conditions with similar characteristics, such as blue nevus, melanocytic nevus, and intradermal nevus, were considered for comparison, but the final experimental dataset used for model training and testing consisted only of melanoma and normal skin images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Malignant Melanoma' or 'Normal Skin' categories.
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates deep learning models (AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, ResNet-18) for binary classification of malignant melanoma versus normal skin. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity. The web application displays prediction results as proportions between normality and melanoma risk.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE dermatology database
datasets_used.description: A subset of images from the MED-NODE dermatology database, specifically sourced from the digital image repository of the Department of Dermatology at the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG) in the Netherlands.

use.training: Used as a source component for the combined dataset used for model development.
use.testing: Used as a source component for the combined dataset used for model evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Custom Collected Dataset (UMCG + Smartphone/Digital Camera Images)
datasets_used.description: A composite dataset created by the researchers, combining 170 images from the MED-NODE database (UMCG) with additional images obtained from smartphone cameras or other digital cameras via medical database searches or general search engines. The dataset was curated by dermatology experts and excludes amelanotic malignant melanoma.

use.training: The training set (286 images) was used for model development and training. Image augmentation (rotation) was applied to this set.
use.testing: The test set (72 images) was used to evaluate the accuracy, precision, specificity, and sensitivity of the deep learning models (AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, ResNet-18).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity) and user satisfaction ratings.

MED-NODE dermatology database + Custom C.evaluation_setting: Binary classification of Malignant Melanoma vs. Normal Skin using three deep learning models with four pre-processing configurations (None, Median filter, Canny edge detection, Median + Canny). Hyperparameters tuned included learning rate (0.1, 0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005), epochs (4, 8, 16, 32), batch size (8, 16, 32), and optimizer (SGDM, ADAM, ADAMW). Image augmentation applied to training set (rotation 0-360 degrees, 10-degree increments).
MED-NODE dermatology database + Custom C.notes: The ResNet-18 model with median filter pre-processing achieved the highest performance (86.11% accuracy). Using median filter improved precision for all models. Canny edge detection alone or combined with median filter did not enhance performance and sometimes decreased it. The best hyperparameter configuration for ResNet-18 was ADAMW optimizer, learning rate 0.0005, 16 epochs, and batch size 16.

Web Application User Evaluation.notes: Users reported high satisfaction. Prediction accuracy scored slightly lower than other categories.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset using transfer learning
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images uploaded as .JPG files)

architecture_details.image_augmentation: Rotation from 0 to 360 degrees in increments of 10 degrees (36 rotations per image)

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Proportions between normality and melanoma risk displayed as a bar chart

preprocessing_techniques: Median filter (kernel size 5x5 pixels) for removing noise
Canny edge detection for identifying lesion edges
Combination of median filter and Canny edge detection
Image augmentation via rotation (0 to 360 degrees in 10-degree increments, 36 rotations per image)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated at fixed angles ranging from 0 to 360 degrees in increments of 10 degrees. Each image was rotated 36 times, resulting in a total of 10,296 images for the training set to avoid overfitting.

conclusions: The ResNet-18 model combined with median filter pre-processing achieved the highest performance for screening malignant melanoma, with 86.11% accuracy, 88.10% precision, 88.10% sensitivity, and 83.33% specificity.
Using a median filter as a pre-processing step improved precision values for all tested deep learning models (AlexNet, MobileNet-V2, and ResNet-18).
Canny edge detection, either alone or combined with a median filter, did not enhance model performance and in some cases decreased it.
The developed web application received high user satisfaction, with an overall rating of 4.48 out of 5 from 30 participants (including general users and general practitioners).
Users rated the web application positively for visual appeal (4.57), usability (4.6), linguistic correctness (4.7), risk assessment and recommendations (4.47), and processing speed (4.5).
Prediction accuracy received a slightly lower user rating (4.2) compared to other usability metrics, though the application was still considered to perform well overall.
The study highlights the potential of deep learning models and median filters to facilitate effective early detection of melanoma risk and improve patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study excludes amelanotic malignant melanoma from the dataset due to lack of pigmentation, limiting the model's applicability to this specific high-risk subtype.
The dataset (358 images) is small and derived from a limited source (MED-NODE database plus custom collection), raising concerns about generalizability and external validation.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely on descriptive metrics.
The web application was evaluated by only 30 participants (25 general users, 5 general practitioners), which is a small sample size for assessing clinical utility and user satisfaction.
The prediction task is binary (Malignant Melanoma vs. Normal Skin), ignoring other differential diagnoses such as nevi or other skin cancers, which limits its clinical scope as a screening tool.
Canny edge detection pre-processing did not improve performance and sometimes decreased it, contrary to common assumptions in image processing for this task.
The study relies on transfer learning with ImageNet-pretrained models without reporting detailed ablation studies or parameter counts, limiting reproducibility of the specific architectural choices.
User-rated prediction accuracy (4.2/5) was lower than other usability metrics, suggesting potential discrepancies between model metrics and user perception of reliability.
